title: Jim Jones and the massacre - Request

transcript.text: Hello, hello. This is the first video I've done for months while riding a bicycle, and first one at this apartment.
So we'll see how it goes.
So this video of your request is from JFL and or VR00.
Hey Kyle, what's up? I'm good. Just riding my bicycle here, thank you.
Next video request. What are your thoughts on Jim Jones and the Jonestown Massacre?
Do you think he had any legitimate messages, or do you think he was just a crazy dude who was well-spoken?
I kind of associate him with people like Hitler on how he can influence so many people with the way he talks in this public image.
Thanks.
Okay, so what do I think about the Jim Jones Massacre?
I watched a 45-minute documentary on it, and then I listened to the entire 45-minute speech conversation that they had before they killed themselves on July 16th, or October 16th, 1979.
And then I listened to him speaking, like in his congregation, between 76 and 79, like 15 minutes of it, and then another section.
So, yeah, so what are my thoughts on Jim Jones and the Massacre? Do I think he had any legitimate messages?
Yeah, he had, you know, every, all these crazy guys, when they say stuff, they say illegitimate stuff.

He would say legitimate cool things, and then he would go back to how he's the chosen one, and how they're going to kill everybody else, and everybody else is bad, and the world sucks, and all this kind of stuff.
And then he'll go back and say something, like the Bible or something, and then he'll go back, and he was, you know, but he was, this guy was, like, scary.
He, um, it's a little bit harder to concentrate while you're moving your legs around.
He, um, what was he saying?
He believed in reincarnation.
I'm going to move this back so I can't see myself.
I get distracted.
He believed in reincarnation.
He said that was a legitimate idea.
And, um, he, uh, what else did he say that was, that was legitimate?
Well, he said that we're all God.
He said, he said, not just Jesus is God, but we're all God.

His first, um, church was in, uh, like Fillmore neighborhood, you know, right there near Haight-Ashbury,
In, um, Haight-Ashbury, in, uh, uh, San Francisco, you know, in, like, the early 70s.
So, right after all the hippie influx over there in 66, 67.

And they're going, and they're being told exactly what to do, because I could hear the person whisper in their ear to say what they said,
Because they couldn't even remember what to say.
And they're all, they're saying the same thing.

Pack them up into a bunch of little pieces and shove them, flush them down the toilet.
So they were just, he latched on to a bunch of really angry people, um, you know, disenfranchised people.
And, uh, I guess there were a whole lot of people like that in those days.

He gave a message that they were going to start a new, um, utopian place in, uh, Guyana.
I guess in Guyana they were looking for more people to populate the country, so they let them go there.
And they got, like, a 2,000, 3,000-acre plot of land.
He'd get all these people to give them all his, all their money.
So when they went down there, they had the money to build the, uh, houses and everything.
And then they couldn't escape.
They didn't have the money to escape.
And they had apparently written off all their family members, too.
So they had nowhere to go.

So, that kind of makes Tom Cruise a really good actor.

But, yeah.
